contents.text: The Anatomy of a Java Program
Every Java program starts with a class definition. Inside that class, you need a 'main' method. Think of the main method as the front door to your house—it is where the execution starts!
Example: public static void main(String[] args) { ... }
Understanding Variables
Variables are like named containers. In Java, you must declare what kind of data the container holds. For example, 'int' is for whole numbers, and 'double' is for decimals.
Remember: Java is strongly typed, meaning you can't put text into a number variable!
Control Flow: If-Else Statements
Decision making in Java is handled by 'if' statements. It allows your program to choose different paths based on whether a condition is true or false.
Syntax: if (condition) { // do this } else { // do that }
Mastering Loops: For and While
Loops prevent you from writing the same code over and over. A 'for' loop is great when you know how many times to repeat, while a 'while' loop runs as long as a condition remains true.
Careful: Always ensure your loop has a way to stop, or you will create an 'infinite loop' that crashes your program!
Methods and Parameters
Methods are reusable blocks of code. You can pass information into them using parameters and get information back using a return type.
Tip: If a method doesn't return anything, its return type must be marked as 'void'.
Introduction to Arrays
An array is a collection of variables of the same type. Think of it like a train with several cars, where each car holds one piece of data.
Note: Arrays in Java are zero-indexed. The first item is at position [0], not [1]!
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
Java is an object-oriented language. This means we organize code into 'Objects' that have attributes (fields) and behaviors (methods).
Concept: A Class is the blueprint, and an Object is the actual house built from that blueprint.
Inheritance and Reusability
Inheritance allows one class to acquire the properties of another. Use the 'extends' keyword to create a subclass.
Example: A 'Dog' class can inherit from an 'Animal' class.
Interfaces and Abstraction
Interfaces define a contract for what a class should do, without specifying how it does it. It's like a to-do list for your classes.
Constraint: A class 'implements' an interface and must provide code for all its methods.
Exception Handling
Errors happen! Java uses 'try-catch' blocks to handle exceptions gracefully so the entire program doesn't stop working when something goes wrong.
Example: try { // risky code } catch (Exception e) { // fix it }
Working with Strings
Strings are objects used to store text. Java provides many methods to manipulate them, such as .length(), .toUpperCase(), and .contains().
Crucial: Strings in Java are immutable. Once created, their value cannot be changed!
